text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Rahman 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
St George’s Hospital, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr A. Rahman, 

Ms Emily Carter, DOB: 22-08-1991, Hospital No: H847392 NHS No. NHS-9021347651  
Flat 3, 27 Oakfield Road, London, SW12 8LP  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification; ongoing evaluation 

Aetiology: 

Unclear; no structural lesion identified on prior MRI; EEG previously non-diagnostic 

Other Medical Problems: 

Iron deficiency anaemia previously treated with oral iron; seasonal allergic rhinitis 

Management Plan: 

- Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; maintain on a single brand.  
- Reinforce adherence and sleep hygiene given caregiving demands.  
- Provide written advice for seizure first aid to family.  
- Arrange repeat EEG with partial sleep deprivation and updated bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs, lamotrigine level, ferritin, vitamin D).  
- DVLA guidance unchanged as she has been free of tonic–clonic seizures for over nine months; she understands to inform us immediately if this changes.  
- Review in four months, sooner if escalation or new features occur.  

History and Current Status: 

She attends today reporting good general health while acting as the primary caregiver for two young children. Night-time sleep can be interrupted, particularly when the youngest wakes in the early hours, and she sometimes compensates with daytime naps when possible. Seizure activity is infrequent. She reports one absence seizure every several week and has been free of tonic-clonic seizures for over nine months. Occasional morning myoclonic jerks continue, though they are mild and do not interfere significantly with daily function. She has not experienced injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence. Triggers she identifies include sleep disruption and missed meals; she denies alcohol or recreational drug use. Medication adherence is generally good; she uses phone reminders and a weekly pill dispenser. No notable adverse effects from lamotrigine aside from occasional mild nausea if taken without food. 

Investigations and Results: 

- Prior MRI brain (2023) reported as normal.  
- Routine EEG (2023) non-diagnostic.  
- Last lamotrigine level (April 2025) within therapeutic range; repeat ordered today alongside routine safety bloods.  

Safety, Lifestyle, and Counselling: 

We reviewed seizure precautions at home, particularly around childcare tasks: avoiding unsupervised bathing of the children, using the rear hob and kettle guards in the kitchen, and sitting on the floor during play if she feels prodromal symptoms. She will continue to aim for consistent bedtimes where possible and to seek support from family to reduce sleep disruption on particularly unsettled nights. I supplied written resources and our clinic contact information for urgent advice. 

Overall Impression: 

Her current pattern remains stable with good day-to-day function and minimal impact from brief events. We will proceed with the planned EEG and monitoring while maintaining her present regimen. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: She reports one absence seizure every several week

Seizure activity is infrequent. She reports one absence seizure every several week and has been free of tonic-clonic seizures for over nine months. Occasional morning myoclonic jerks continue, though they are mild and do not interfere significantly with daily function

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states current seizures include absence seizures and occasional myoclonic jerks. It explicitly quantifies: "one absence seizure every several week". Tonic–clonic seizures have been absent for over nine months but other seizures continue, so she is not seizure-free overall. The myoclonic jerks are described as occasional without a quantifiable rate. Applying the rule to select the highest determinable frequency, use the quantified absence seizure rate. This maps to 1 per multiple week due to the vague interval "several week".
seizure_frequency_number: She reports one absence seizure every several week
reference: Seizure activity is infrequent. She reports one absence seizure every several week and has been free of tonic-clonic seizures for over nine months. Occasional morning myoclonic jerks continue, though they are mild and do not interfere significantly with daily function
